data.title: Wall Street turns red after the Nasdaq's morning 2% rally
data.description:
U.S. stocks gave back early gains on Thursday, erasing a morning rally that had pushed the Nasdaq Composite above 2%.

The tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was last down by 1.3%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) had pushed down by 0.8% and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) dipped by 0.3%. 

From a sector-by-sector point of view, three of the 11 S&P segments find themselves up in positive territory and are led by Consumer Staples. Going in the other direction, Info Tech has been the weakest performer thus far.

As for stocks that were on the move, Nvidia was last -2.2% after adding more than $200B to its market cap with its post-earnings rally, nearing the $200/share level it lost early this month.

Thursday's spotlight was also on the September nonfarm payrolls report, which was initially supposed to come out on October 3. However, the longest U.S. government shutdown in history derailed that release.

The data showed the U.S. labor market added 119K jobs in September, easily outstripping the consensus of 50K. The unemployment rate ticked up to 4.4% in the month, a tad higher than the expected 4.3%.

“Thursday's jobs report was much stronger than expected and it's possible that the Federal Reserve may take more of a wait and see approach to rates in December, especially since there is still uncertainty over the economic data, which has only first started to come back online after the drought from the government shutdown. Thursday's jobs report suggests that the labor market is not as weak as feared,” Alexander Guiliano, chief investment officer, Resonate Wealth Partners stated.

Moreover, Treasury yields shifted lower as the longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) moved down by 4 basis points to 4.09% and the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) pushed down by 4 basis points to 3.55%.

data.title: B. Riley calls out an attractive entry point on Genius Sports for investors
data.description:
B. Riley Securities defended its bullish view on Genius Sports (GENI) on Thursday, calling the recent sector-driven weakness for shares a compelling entry point for investors due to the strong fundamentals in place.

Analyst Josh Nichols said the sports data company's strong operating performance was amplified by several sector headwinds facing its B2C customers, including structural hold deterioration and general prediction market threat concerns. "The market is incorrectly treating GENI as a transaction-based sportsbook proxy when the business is fundamentally a data and technology platform provider with contracted recurring revenue, minimal betting outcome exposure, and exclusive content relationships locked through 2029/2030," he advised.

Looking ahead, Nichols and his team are optimistic about Genius Sports (GENI) heading into the December 3 analyst day event in New York City. The expectation is that management will articulate a multi-year roadmap demonstrating how the company reaches $1 billion+ annual revenue by 2027-2028 with EBITDA margins progressing toward management's 30%+ target, reestablishing GENI's differentiation as a lower-volatility platform technology company with a significant competitivemoat. 

Importantly, B. Riley sees prediction market concerns pivoting to opportunity. "While the rapid growth of sports betting through prediction markets has been a significant headwind for gaming shares, we believe the nascent category could represent a meaningful market expansion opportunity as regulatory clarity emerges," assessed Nichols. As the category matures, the need for official league data, marks and logos, and integrity solutions is seen intensifying, which should work to Genius Sports (GENI) favor.

B. Riley has a Buy rating on Genius Sports (GENI) and a price target of $14.

Shares  of Genius Sports (GENI) were up 2.2% in Thursday afternoon action.

data.title: Drugmakers to develop pharma manufacturing training site in Virginia
data.description:
• AstraZeneca (AZN), Eli Lilly (LLY), and Merck have signed a memorandum of understanding with the Virginia Innovation Partnership Corp. to develop a pharmaceutical manufacturing training facility. 
  • The drugmakers have pledged $120M in workforce investment for the project.
  • The Virginia Center for Advanced Pharmaceutical Manufacturing will work with some of the state's top universities, including the University of Virginia, Virginia Commonwealth University, Virginia Tech, Old Dominion University, and James Madison University.
  • "The Center will prepare students and workers for the next generation of high-quality, high-demand advanced manufacturing jobs in biopharma through hands-on GMP-simulated training, industry credentials and certifications, and employer apprenticeships and internships," according to a news release.
  • Earlier this year, AstraZeneca, Eli Lilly, and Merck announced multi-billion-dollar manufacturing facility investments in Virginia.

data.title: Top retail Quant stocks as Walmart is S&P 500’s top gainer
data.description:
The retail sector is showing strong performance on Thursday, despite volatility in the markets, with Walmart (WMT) leading the gains among S&P 500 stocks with a nearly 6.4% jump on Thursday after the company rose full year sales growth outlook.

Using Seeking Alpha’s proprietary Quant Ratings system, we’ve identified the top retail stocks that demonstrate the most promising investment characteristics according to a sophisticated trading algorithm.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant Ratings have consistently outperformed the market, with their very bullish recommendations beating the S&P 500 (SP500) by 1,755% compared to 379% from January 2010 to June 2021.

The Quant Model identifies stocks that collectively hold the best investment characteristics across five key factors: growth, profitability, value, momentum, and rising analysts’ earnings estimates. This automated stock selection process efficiently sorts through vast amounts of data beyond human capacity, providing investors with a data-driven approach to stock selection.

Top retail stocks by Quant rating:

  1. Citi Trends (CTRN) - Strong Buy rating of 4.84, showing significant market resilience with year-to-date growth of 161.47%.

data.title: BBB Foods signals runway for 14,000 stores as same-store sales climb 17.9% while accelerating expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: BBB Foods Inc. (TBBB) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Kamal Hatoum reported "another quarter of exceptional growth, outperforming other listed players," citing the opening of 131 net new stores, bringing the total to 3,162, and two new distribution centers, now totaling 18. Hatoum emphasized, "Same-store sales grew by 17.9%. Total revenues increased by 36.7% to reach MXN 20.3 billion."
  • Hatoum highlighted, "We have significant runway with room for no less than 14,000 3B stores in Mexico," and pointed to brand equity and talent as key drivers: "Our brand equity continues to strengthen...We continue to invest in talent."
  • CFO Eduardo Pizzuto stated, "Sales expenses as a percentage of revenue increased from 10.1% to 10.2%." He commented on margin progression: "EBITDA increased 43.6% to reach 5.8%, driven by sales and margin growth and operational efficiency."
  • Pizzuto added, "Our business model generates significant negative working capital. And in turn, we generate significant cash flow from the changes in negative working capital."
  • Hatoum noted a board decision: "Our Board of Directors decided in its last meeting not to make additional reserves for our equity incentive plan for the year 2026."

Outlook

  • Hatoum stated, "We are hitting or exceeding our targets with same-store sales that stand out versus industry."
  • On future sales, Hatoum said, "I can say with a high degree of confidence based on all the work and research we've done that we see no reason why same-store sales would be any weaker than this year."
  • Pizzuto commented, "We'll provide guidance for the next year in our next call."

Financial Results

  • Hatoum reported, "EBITDA reported a loss of MXN 404 million. If we exclude our noncash share-based payments, then EBITDA increased by 43.6% and reached a positive MXN 1.2 billion."
  • Operating cash flow for the first nine months of 2025 was "MXN 3 billion or a 30% increase year-on-year."
  • Pizzuto detailed, "We can see, for example, that in September '25, we had MXN 7.8 billion compared to a negative working capital of MXN 5.4 billion in the third quarter of '24, excluding IPO proceeds."

Q&A

  • Robert Ford, BofA Securities: Asked about gross margin, value proposition, and market share. Hatoum responded that scaling is "lowering your purchasing costs and...increasing your logistics efficiency," and that value improvements are driving new and existing customer growth, with significant room for further penetration.
  • Joseph Giordano, JPMorgan: Queried faster maturation of new store vintages and return levels. Hatoum replied newer stores "mature faster" due to stronger brand recognition, and performance is "just as good, if not better" than older cohorts. Pizzuto added, "We have not seen maturation yet. Even for our 2005 vintage, we continue to see very strong increases."
  • Alvaro Garcia, BTG Pactual: Asked about sales expenses and regional ramp-up. Pizzuto attributed sales expense comps to D&A timing, forecasting more favorable numbers next quarter. Hatoum noted "faster ramp-ups...across the board" with no notable geographic differences.
  • Alejandro Fuchs, Itaú: Asked about competition and same-store sales drivers. Hatoum observed no change in competitive landscape. Pizzuto said same-store sales growth comes from "more transactions in the stores" and "more products in the basket."
  • Héctor Maya López, Scotiabank: Probed sustainability of same-store sales and margin decisions. Hatoum expressed high confidence in continued robust sales, but cautioned on quarter-to-quarter volatility for margins, which are managed by "elasticity testing in the market."
  • Alexandre Namioka, Morgan Stanley: Asked about perishables category rollout. Hatoum said tests are "extremely positive," but launch will await full operational readiness.
  • Irma Sgarz, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about customer journey, demographic adaptation, and operating leverage. Hatoum described a journey from basic goods to broader categories, supported by a "100% money back guarantee." Pizzuto emphasized operating leverage is visible in older stores, but rapid expansion masks it at the consolidated level.
  • Alexander Wright, Jefferies: Asked about HR constraints and CapEx. Hatoum said talent remains the gating factor but is being managed through long-term planning. Pizzuto expects CapEx to be "very close to the number that we projected late last year around the MXN 3.7 billion."
  • Santiago Alvarez, Summit Management: Asked about sales mix and private label penetration. Pizzuto noted older stores are reaching "around, let's say, 7% EBITDA margins" and private label penetration "continues to evolve."
  • Gullie Arshad, Summit Management: Inquired about interest from larger players or a potential bear hug. Hatoum answered, "not to my knowledge."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on expansion returns, margin sustainability, and competitive landscape, pressing for details on future growth and operational leverage, with a slightly positive tone.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone, frequently referencing robust results and long-term planning. Hatoum stated, "the future looks bright."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management displayed similar confidence, with management reiterating operational success and expansion capability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter emphasized acceleration in store openings and distribution center expansion, with 131 new stores and 2 new DCs, compared to 142 stores opened in Q2.
  • Same-store sales growth ticked up to 17.9% from 17.7% in the prior quarter.
  • Revenue growth moderated slightly from 38.3% to 36.7%, while EBITDA margin, after adjustments for share-based payments, improved.
  • Analysts continued to focus on return on investment, margin evolution, and competitive dynamics, while management maintained emphasis on brand equity, operational efficiency, and long-term growth runway.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified talent acquisition and development as an ongoing constraint but asserted robust long-term planning to address it.
  • Supply chain risks are mitigated through three-year planning cycles.
  • Analysts probed for risks related to competition and market saturation, but management consistently emphasized significant remaining market potential and lack of competitive pressures.

Final Takeaway

BBB Foods Inc. continues to highlight exceptional growth, with rapid store and distribution center expansion, strong same-store sales, and operational improvements. Management asserts that significant market opportunity remains in Mexico, with a long-term target of 14,000 stores, and expects robust sales and margin improvement trends to persist, supported by brand strength, talent density, and disciplined execution. Investors are presented with a company executing on an aggressive growth strategy while maintaining operational discipline and a positive outlook for the near future.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Record number of shoppers expected to turn out during Thanksgiving weekend -- NRF
data.description:
Shrinking wallets? Not this holiday season where forecasts from the National Retail Federation ((NRF)) point to another record-breaking Black Friday.

In their latest consumer survey, the NRF expect a record 186.9 million people will shop from Thanksgiving Day through Cyber Monday, breaking the previous record set last year by 3 million people.

Black Friday remains the most popular day to shop and capitalize on great deals with 70% of those polled (130.4 million) expected to shop the Friday after Thanksgiving, followed by Cyber Monday (40%). Small Business Saturday is expected to bring out 36% of those polled with the majority citing support of local businesses.

“Of those planning to take advantage of sales, over half say it’s because the deals are too good to pass up. Others point to the tradition or simply say they like to start their holiday shopping during the long weekend,” NRF Prosper Insights & Analytics Vice President of Strategy, Phil Rist said.

NRF expects shoppers to shell out more than a trillion dollars on holiday spending this year using debt cards (44%), credit cards (27%), and cash (16%) with a budget of $890 on gifts and other seasonal items. Fifty-nine percent of this year’s shoppers will embrace the digital trend by using their digital wallets and apps for purchases, in line with recent years.

This year, families with kids are budgeting an extra $33 for gifts, with little ones asking Santa for Legos, Barbie dolls, and Hot Wheels.

The most popular item for gifts this year is clothing and accessories, taking the top spot from electronics as the most popular gift item in 2024. Gift cards and toys remain a popular gift option, according to 43% and 32% of those polled by the NRF, respectively. Consumers are expected to spend $29.1 billion on gift cards, an increase of 1.7% from last year.

A trend gaining traction is secondhand gifts -- not to be confused with re-gifting. The NRF found that more than half of those polled would consider buying a secondhand or “upcycled” gift to remain within their holiday budget and get a greater value for their money. Some popular secondhand gifts include books, accessories, and home décor.

Related tickers: Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN), Mattel (NASDAQ:MAT), Hasbro (NASDAQ:HAS), Best Buy (NYSE:BBY), Walmart (NYSE:WMT), Target (NYSE:TGT), GameStop (NYSE:GME), Nike (NKE), The RealReal (REAL), Abercrombie & Fitch (NYSE:ANF), Gap (NYSE:GAP), Victoria’s Secret (NYSE:VSCO), Ross Stores (NASDAQ:ROST), TJX Companies (NYSE:TJX), Deckers Outdoor (NYSE:DECK), DICK’s Sporting Goods (NYSE:DKS), Bath & Body Works (NYSE:BBWI), Ulta (NASDAQ:ULTA), Five Below (NASDAQ:FIVE), Burlington Stores (NYSE:BURL), Urban Outfitters (NASDAQ:URBN), SPDR S&P Retail ETF (NYSEARCA:XRT), Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (NYSEARCA:XLY).

data.title: Ellington Credit signals readiness for unsecured debt issuance amid $380M CLO portfolio milestone
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ellington Credit Company (EARN) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Laurence Penn, CEO, stated that "Ellington Credit delivered another strong quarter" as the company's CLO portfolio ramped up and net investment income rose, benefiting from "several CLO note redemptions at par on discounted purchases as well as our robust trading activity with more than 90 distinct CLO trades executed during the quarter." He announced, "Ellington Credit Company achieved full dividend coverage from net investment income in September, underscoring the earnings power of our portfolio as we get closer to being fully invested."
  • CEO Penn detailed a "deliberate move up in credit quality," with approximately 70% of net CLO purchases over the past two quarters in mezzanine debt tranches, emphasizing their "compelling combination of yield and downside protection."
  • Penn highlighted a strategic shift: "We gained similar exposures, but at better pricing in secondary market acquisitions of longer duration equity" and stressed active trading as a core advantage given EARN's "relatively modest size with $225 million of equity to invest."
  • Christopher Smernoff, CFO, stated, "For calendar Q3, we reported GAAP net income of $0.11 per share and net investment income of $0.23 per share. The weighted average GAAP yield for the quarter on our CLO portfolio was 15.5%."
  • Smernoff provided further detail: "In total, the CLO portfolio increased by 20% to $380 million. During the quarter, we made new purchases totaling $160 million, 62% of that in CLO debt and 38% in CLO equity and sold $29 million of CLOs, consistent with our active trading approach."
  • Gregory Borenstein, Portfolio Manager, noted, "We saw an uptick in CLO trades for EARN from 79 in Q2 to 92 in Q3, emphasizing our trading-focused flexible approach."

Outlook

  • CEO Penn indicated that "our likely next step is to raise long-term unsecured notes, which we hope to complete in the coming weeks, market conditions permitting. We expect this additional capital to be accretive to both net investment income and GAAP earnings."
  • Management expects "elevated repricing activity and ongoing credit dispersion to continue to create opportunities for outperformance through active portfolio management."
  • Borenstein emphasized continued focus on mezzanine tranches and secondary market opportunities, stating, "We expect to continue to see compelling special situations, especially in the secondary market."

Financial Results

  • CFO Smernoff reported "GAAP net income of $0.11 per share and net investment income of $0.23 per share. The weighted average GAAP yield for the quarter on our CLO portfolio was 15.5%."
  • The CLO portfolio increased by 20% to $380 million as of September 30, with 51% in CLO equity and 14% in European CLO investments.
  • Cash and cash equivalents totaled $20.1 million at quarter end. Net asset value (NAV) was $5.99 per share, and NAV-based total return for the quarter was 9.6% annualized.
  • Credit hedges expanded to $90 million in high-yield CDX bond equivalents, or about 40% of NAV at quarter end, with further increases to over $150 million as of October 31.

Q&A

  • Crispin Love, Piper Sandler, asked about the significant increase in credit hedges; CEO Penn explained, "Just the increase in the size of the credit hedges was mostly a function of just the increase in the portfolio size and the increase in the leverage in terms of just on an absolute dollar basis in terms of how much debt we have through repo."
  • Gregory Borenstein added, "As we've increased financing on CLO mezzanine position since we've tended to favor those, we've needed to add more protection in these drawdown scenarios from a liquidity point of view."
  • Love questioned the decrease in CLO portfolio in October; Borenstein responded, "October is a quarterly payment date, too. So the equity portfolio will have distributions and generally a bit of a markdown in prices."
  • Douglas Harter, UBS, asked about leverage appetite and unsecured debt; CEO Penn stated, "We are constrained by all of the restrictions of the '40 Act... So a little less than 2:1 leverage. Again, that's also given our current 2:1 asset to equity leverage."
  • Eric Hagen, BTIG, inquired about future CLO supply and AI-related credits; Borenstein replied, "I think some of it will depend on what we see with new issue loan supply... you will find that a portion of the market will be affected, sometimes good, sometimes bad, by what AI may ultimately end up bringing."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on risk management, portfolio hedging, and leverage, with a neutral to slightly cautious tone, seeking clarification on hedging rationale and market exposures.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently reiterating the efficacy of their active trading strategy and hedging approach, as evidenced by phrases like "we feel confident about our ability to maintain dividend coverage over the long term."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, sentiment from both analysts and management was more assured, with less emphasis on liquidity concerns and more on strategic growth and risk mitigation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter showed a marked shift from ramping and redeploying capital post-conversion to focusing on portfolio optimization, mezzanine debt, and secondary market opportunities.
  • Guidance language evolved from cautious optimism about deployment and dividend coverage to explicit confidence, backed by "full dividend coverage from net investment income in September."
  • The analysts' focus shifted from liquidity and deployment to risk management and leverage appetite.
  • Key metrics improved, with the CLO portfolio increasing from $250 million to $380 million, and NAV stabilizing at $5.99 per share.
  • Management's tone transitioned from cautious post-conversion to confident in active trading and hedging strategies.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted "volatility and credit dispersion" as ongoing risks, noting that "recent Tricolor and First Brands bankruptcies... underscores that the corporate credit markets are also vulnerable to idiosyncratic volatility and credit dispersion."
  • CEO Penn detailed the expansion of the credit hedging portfolio to "roughly $90 million of high-yield CDX bond equivalents by the end of the quarter," emphasizing its role in downside protection.
  • Borenstein noted, "We are continuing to find the secondary markets far more compelling than primary markets," but cautioned about the impact of AI and defaults on CLO equity returns.

Final Takeaway

Ellington Credit Company reported a strong quarter marked by significant CLO portfolio growth, strategic rotation into mezzanine debt, and robust active trading. Management achieved full dividend coverage from net investment income, expanded credit hedges as a risk buffer, and signaled plans to raise long-term unsecured notes to support future growth. With a balanced portfolio, disciplined hedging, and a focus on special situations in the secondary market, the company expressed confidence in its ability to deliver resilient returns amid ongoing market volatility and credit dispersion.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: CDC changes stance on vaccine link to autism claims
data.description:
The CDC altered a webpage Wednesday dealing with autism and vaccines to state that the idea that vaccines don't cause autism "is not an evidence-based claim."

The page notes that "studies have not ruled out the possibility that infant vaccines cause autism," adding that "Studies supporting a link have been ignored by health authorities."

The development was first reported by The New York Times.

The site now contains information often cited by HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., including that the rise in autism diagnoses since the 1980s coincides with more childhood vaccines administered.

"Though the cause of autism is likely to be multi-factorial, the scientific foundation to rule out one potential contributor entirely has not been established," the CDC now says.

The page still contains a header reading "Vaccines do not cause Autism." A note at the bottom states that the header is remaining following an agreement with Senate Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions Committee Chair Bill Cassidy (R-La.), a medical doctor who has sparred with RFK Jr. 

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Maximus projects $8.10 EPS and $475M free cash flow for fiscal 2026 while advancing AI and federal growth initiatives
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Maximus (MMS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Bruce L. Caswell, President and CEO, highlighted that "revenue and profitability came in higher than projected, reflecting both the strength of our core operations and the disciplined execution of our strategy throughout the year." Caswell emphasized a 3.9% organic growth rate for the consolidated business, with U.S. Federal Services delivering 12.1% organic growth and outside the U.S. segment delivering 4.1% organic growth.
  • Caswell stated, "contractual relationships remained stable and secure throughout the year, with cancellations or impacts at just 0.5% of fiscal year 2025 revenue, a figure that is unchanged from our prior earnings call." He further noted the company’s confidence in future growth, driven by investments in AI and technology-enabled government solutions.
  • Strategic priorities for fiscal 2026 include expanding in U.S. federal markets, capitalizing on opportunities from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, and deploying AI and automation across operations. Caswell shared details on a new $86 million Air Force contract and the company’s first cooperative research and development agreement (CRADA) to bolster intellectual property and national security capabilities.
  • "Our Total Experience Management or TXM solution, which I've mentioned on prior calls, is a FedRAMP secure, modular, flexible, scalable and configurable platform that helps enable federal agencies to deliver smarter citizen-centric services," said Caswell. He also highlighted 30 AI-related deployments planned or in progress, varying from pilots to full-scale implementations.
  • Caswell indicated that signed awards for fiscal 2025 totaled $4.7 billion, with a book-to-bill of approximately 0.9x for the trailing 12 months and a pipeline at September 30 of $51.3 billion.
  • David Mutryn, CFO, stated, "revenue totaled $5.43 billion. This equates to organic growth of 3.9% over the prior year. From an earnings standpoint, the full year adjusted EBITDA margin was 12.9% and adjusted earnings per share were $7.36." Mutryn also reported, "free cash flow for the full fiscal year 2025" was $366 million and highlighted a focus on debt paydown and share repurchases totaling $457 million.

Outlook

  • Fiscal 2026 revenue is projected to be between $5.225 billion and $5.425 billion, with a midpoint of $5.325 billion. Adjusted EPS is projected to be between $7.95 and $8.25, with a midpoint of $8.10 per share. Free cash flow is guided to a midpoint of $475 million, representing about 30% year-over-year growth.
  • Mutryn explained, "The midpoint of $5.325 billion of revenue reflects our current view of volume dynamics on some of our variable work." He added, "the $8.10 midpoint of adjusted EPS guidance reflects ongoing margin expansion and 10% growth over fiscal 2025."
  • Management anticipates that some excess volumes in fiscal 2025 will not recur in fiscal 2026, resulting in a year-over-year revenue headwind of approximately 3%, partially offset by 1% organic growth. Margin guidance has improved to 13.7% for adjusted EBITDA.

Financial Results

  • For fiscal year 2025, revenue increased 2.4% to $5.43 billion. The U.S. Federal Services segment registered $3.07 billion in revenue, with a 15.3% operating income margin. The U.S. Services segment posted $1.76 billion in revenue and a 9.7% operating margin, impacted by $16 million in severance costs.
  • Outside the U.S., revenue fell to $600 million, primarily due to prior divestitures, but organic growth reached 4.1% and the segment margin improved to 3.7%. Cash flows from operating activities totaled $429 million, and fourth quarter free cash flow was $642 million. Net leverage stood at 1.5x at year-end.
  • Mutryn noted, "severance charges totaling approximately $16 million...had a more pronounced effect on the operating margins of the U.S. Services segment." He expects these cost management efforts to support margin expansion in fiscal 2026.

Q&A

  • Will, CJS Securities: Asked about the drivers behind the strong EBITDA margin guidance for 2026. Mutryn responded, "all 3 (segments) are actually slightly higher than where they finished fiscal year '25...the continued deployment of technology and automation as well as cost management."
  • Will, CJS Securities: Inquired about segment level revenue growth. Mutryn explained that both U.S. Federal and U.S. Services may see mild contraction, with more erosion on the federal side due to overperformance in 2025. However, the federal segment also has the strongest near-term pipeline.
  • Will, CJS Securities: Asked about the impact of the government shutdown. Caswell answered, "We really don't anticipate any negative impacts on our delivery on our contract portfolio in Q1 FY '26. Nearly all of our programs were deemed essential services by the government."
  • Will, CJS Securities: Sought details on capital allocation and M&A priorities. Caswell said the focus remains on disciplined M&A in the U.S. federal market, particularly defense and national security, with priorities on customer relationships, technical capabilities, and business systems.
  • Will, CJS Securities: Requested an update on the One Big Beautiful Bill Act. Caswell stated, "there's been no real update from a policy perspective and not surprisingly, no regulatory updates either...the SNAP payment error rate issue...can lead to a significant financial lift for states who don't bring their error rates down below 6%." He described ongoing engagement with states, demonstrations, and technology solutions to address error rates.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a positive and constructive tone, seeking clarity on margin drivers, segment trends, capital allocation, and policy-related opportunities. No adversarial exchanges or pressing skepticism were evident.
  • Management's tone in prepared remarks was confident, with Caswell stating, "We are proud of what we accomplished, and we are energized by the momentum we're carrying into fiscal 2026." During Q&A, management remained detailed and optimistic, emphasizing their strategic advantages and readiness for future opportunities.
  • Compared to the prior quarter, sentiment remained positive, with management continuing to express confidence in growth initiatives and analysts focusing on execution details and future drivers.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for fiscal 2026 is now formalized with revenue midpoint at $5.325 billion and adjusted EPS midpoint at $8.10, compared to early color in Q3, which expected flat or slightly lower revenue and improved margins.
  • Margin guidance has increased, with adjusted EBITDA margin projected at 13.7%, above the high end of the previous target range. Management cited ongoing technology and cost initiatives.
  • Analysts' focus shifted from clarifying the timing and impact of policy-driven growth in Q3 to seeking more granular details on margin improvement and segment performance in Q4.
  • Management's tone has been consistently confident, now reinforced by specific contract wins, AI deployment progress, and a larger pipeline. Analysts’ sentiment remained positive but increasingly sought specificity on execution and risk management.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted that some revenue headwinds are anticipated in fiscal 2026 due to the non-recurrence of excess volumes and unpredictable natural disaster support.
  • Caswell noted the possibility of delayed procurement activity and the need for states to address SNAP payment error rates to avoid federal funding reductions.
  • Delays in customer payments and the risk of future government shutdowns were acknowledged, but management indicated minimal anticipated impacts due to the essential nature of Maximus’ services and diversified contract base.

Final Takeaway

Maximus enters fiscal 2026 with formal guidance targeting $5.325 billion in revenue, $8.10 adjusted EPS, and $475 million in free cash flow, underpinned by strong customer relationships, disciplined cost management, and strategic investments in AI and federal markets. Management underscores the durability of its contract portfolio and sees significant future opportunity from policy-driven initiatives and technology adoption, particularly positioning the company for potential accelerated growth in fiscal 2027 and beyond.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Cleveland Fed Pres. Hammack sees inflation as ongoing concern despite labor market softening
data.description:
Cleveland Federal Reserve Bank President Beth Hammack believes the Fed must maintain a somewhat restrictive monetary policy stance despite signs of softening in the labor market, as inflation pressures remain significant.

In an interview with CNBC, Hammack emphasized that recent economic data presents a challenging balancing act for monetary policymakers trying to fulfill their dual mandate.

“We are seeing some signs of softening in the labor market, but we’re still seeing elevated signs of inflation,” Hammack said.

She noted that while the recent jobs report showed mixed results, the Cleveland Fed is also monitoring other indicators such as ADP jobs data and layoff notices, which have been “moving up slightly.”

Hammack highlighted the persistent impact of inflation on households, particularly among workers in her district. “The pressure from inflation is still really significant and that they’re using their paychecks just for food and housing and energy,” she said, adding that consumers “really don’t have anything left over at the end of the month.”

Regarding monetary policy, Hammack believes current rates are “barely restrictive, if at all” and stressed that “we need to maintain a somewhat restrictive stance to bring inflation back down to our 2% objective.”

She noted that some inflation indicators, particularly in core services excluding housing, have been “trending upwards not downwards” over recent months.

The Cleveland Fed president also addressed financial stability concerns, particularly in private credit markets and emerging technologies like AI.

While acknowledging positive aspects of private credit, including “reasonably stable funding” with “seven-to-ten-year lockups,” she expressed concern about connections to the banking system through “significant growth in lending” from banks to private credit entities.

When asked about interest rates, Hammack indicated that current rates seem “right around a neutral rate” and “fairly restrictive.”

Drawing from her market experience in the 1990s, she remarked that the current yield curve around 4% represents “actually pretty modest interest rates” compared to historical standards.

Earlier on Thursday, Hammack prepared a speech for the Cleveland Fed's 2025 Financial Stability Conference, where she warned that easing monetary policy too quickly or too much could also endanger financial stability.

More on the US economy

  • Treasury Market Scuttles Hope For December Rate Cut, Doubts January Cut, After Hawkish Fed Minutes
  • Fed minutes show divide in views on direction of policy at October meeting
  • Delinquencies, charge-offs rise as consumer sentiment weakens: October Credit Pulse
